title: Fine-Tuning: Teaching a Model a Narrower Behavior
thesis: Fine-tuning reshapes a model's learned behavior by continuing training on targeted examples, making it useful for stable, narrow tasks, but it is not a substitute for clear instructions, good data, or ongoing evaluation.

claims.claim: Fine-tuning changes a model's learned behavior by continuing training on targeted examples, rather than changing what the model sees at runtime.

evidence.snippet: Fine-tuning lets you get more out of the models available through the API by providing higher quality results than prompt engineering alone, training with more examples than can fit in a prompt.

counterevidence.summary: Some providers now blur the line by allowing runtime examples, retrieval, and cached weights; the practical boundary between fine-tuning and prompting can depend on the platform.

claims.claim: Fine-tuning is a form of transfer learning: it adapts a general model to a narrower task using additional examples.

evidence.snippet: Transfer learning and domain adaptation refer to the situation where what has been learned in one setting is exploited to improve generalization in another setting.

counterevidence.summary: Not every fine-tuning procedure follows the classic transfer-learning setup; some methods train on a broad mixture of tasks rather than a single narrow target domain.

claims.claim: Fine-tuning works best when the task is narrow, the desired outputs are consistent, and high-quality labeled examples are available.

evidence.snippet: Fine-tuning is a training approach where a pretrained model is trained on a smaller, task-specific dataset to adapt it for a particular downstream task.

LoRA freezes the pretrained model weights and injects trainable rank decomposition matrices into each layer of the Transformer architecture, greatly reducing the number of trainable parameters for downstream tasks.

counterevidence.summary: Emerging techniques such as instruction tuning train on very broad mixtures and still improve across many tasks, so narrowness is a useful heuristic rather than a strict requirement.

claims.claim: Fine-tuning can bake in errors, biases, or brittle patterns from the training data, so it must be paired with evaluation and clear limits.

evidence.snippet: Models trained with RLHF can become more brittle and can exploit distributional shifts or optimize the proxy objective in unintended ways.

counterevidence.summary: Careful data curation, holdout evaluation, and human oversight can reduce these risks substantially; the claim is about necessary caution, not inevitability.

sources.title: Goodfellow, Bengio, and Courville: Deep Learning
LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback

agentInstructions: Use claim IDs as the retrieval unit.
Treat maturity=seed as an explicit uncertainty marker.
Do not present fine-tuning as a substitute for good data, clear goals, or human review.
When summarizing, preserve the plain-language-first, technical-depth-later structure.
